1.synthesis: At Iconium, Paul and Barnabas enter the synagogue and speak in such a way that a great number of Jews and Greeks believe (Acts 14:1). Unbelieving Jews stir up the Gentiles and poison their minds against the brothers (Acts 14:2). They remain for a long time, speaking boldly for the Lord, who bears witness to the word of his grace by granting signs and wonders through their hands (Acts 14:3). The city is divided; an attempt is made to mistreat and stone them, and they flee to the cities of Lycaonia (Acts 14:4–7).
1.historical_context: Iconium (modern Konya, Turkey) was a prosperous city in the Phrygian region with a significant Jewish community and mixed Greco-Roman population. Paul's second letter to Timothy recalls that 'persecutions came to me at Antioch, at Iconium, and at Lystra' (2 Timothy 3:11)—the Iconium experience was memorable enough to be cited decades later. The 'long time' they spent at Iconium (v.3) despite opposition demonstrates that mission is not merely reactive to opposition but persists through it. Signs and wonders authenticate the proclaimed word, not as mere displays of power but as confirmation that the Lord backs his messenger.
1.literary_structure: The Iconium episode is brief but structurally complete: initial success → opposition that poisons minds → sustained witness despite opposition → signs and wonders → city divided → threat of violence → flight. This compressed pattern will repeat in city after city. Luke's three-city sequence (Pisidian Antioch, Iconium, Lystra) demonstrates the escalation of opposition: from verbal rejection (Antioch) to active conspiracy (Iconium) to actual stoning (Lystra). The Lord's bearing witness 'to the word of his grace' (v.3) identifies the signs as validation of the message content, not performance.
1.theological_application: The city being 'divided' (v.4) by the gospel is the fulfillment of Jesus' prediction in Luke 12:51: 'not peace but division.' The division is not the church's failure but its inevitability—the gospel makes a claim that demands response, and different responses in the same community produce conflict. Paul and Barnabas's willingness to remain 'a long time' despite the poisoned atmosphere models persistence in mission rather than quick departure when opposition arises. Flight comes only when violence threatens bodily harm, not when social hostility makes the mission uncomfortable.
1.christology: The Lord bearing witness 'to the word of his grace' through signs and wonders performed by Paul and Barnabas (v.3) is the risen Christ actively endorsing his apostles' mission. The phrase 'word of his grace' (logos tēs charitos autou) appears also in Acts 20:32—it is Luke's characterization of the gospel as fundamentally a word of gracious invitation, not law. The risen Christ's ongoing authentication of the mission through signs is the continuation of his earthly ministry by other means: the same works that characterized his Galilean ministry now accompany his apostolic emissaries.

8.synthesis: At Lystra, Paul sees a man crippled from birth who has faith to be healed—Paul says in a loud voice, 'Stand upright on your feet,' and the man leaps up and walks (Acts 14:8–10). The crowds, seeing this, shout in the Lycaonian language: 'The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!'—calling Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes (Acts 14:11–12). The priest of Zeus brings oxen and garlands to offer sacrifice at the gate (Acts 14:13). Paul and Barnabas tear their garments and rush in, crying: 'Men, why are you doing these things? We also are men, of like nature with you, and we bring you good news, that you should turn from these vain things to a living God, who made the heaven and the earth' (Acts 14:14–15). God did not leave himself without witness—he gave rains and fruitful seasons and satisfaction (Acts 14:17). Even with these words they scarcely restrain the crowd.
8.historical_context: A local legend recorded in Ovid's Metamorphoses (8.626–720) told of Zeus and Hermes visiting the Phrygian region disguised as mortals; only Philemon and Baucis welcomed them, while others were destroyed. Lystran locals would have been primed to interpret a miracle-working pair through this lens and anxious not to repeat the error of their ancestors. Barnabas is called Zeus (the greater deity, presumably because of his physical presence) while Paul is called Hermes (the divine spokesman). Paul's counter-proclamation uses natural theology—creation as divine witness—appropriate for a Gentile audience with no Jewish background; it parallels his Areopagus speech in chapter 17 but at a more basic level.
8.literary_structure: The Lystra healing miracle (vv.8–10) deliberately echoes the Temple Gate Beautiful healing (Acts 3:1–10): the same disability (crippled from birth), the same perception of faith, the same leap and walking. But the crowd response is entirely different—instead of Jewish praise to God, Gentile apotheosis of the healers. The speech (vv.14–17) addresses a pagan audience with no scriptural framework, using creation-based natural theology rather than Davidic-messianic proof texts. The restraint of the crowd (v.18) is with difficulty—Luke signals that the underlying religious energy is powerful and barely redirected.
8.theological_application: Paul and Barnabas's horrified rejection of divine honor—tearing garments, running into the crowd, denying divine nature—models the church's refusal to accept worship directed at human agents of God. The temptation to accept the role of 'divine figure' when performing miraculous acts is real; the apostolic response is consistent deflection toward the living God who does these things. The natural theology of vv.16–17 demonstrates that the church's proclamation to people without scriptural background must begin with what they can already apprehend—the creation's testimony to its Creator—before moving to the specific gospel of Jesus Christ.
8.christology: The irony of the Lystra scene is that the crowd almost worship the wrong people in the right place (Lystra was where Barnabas and Paul healed in Jesus' name) while missing entirely the one who should receive worship. The healing is performed in Jesus' name; the glory is nearly misdirected to his servants. Paul's speech points to 'the living God, who made heaven and earth' (Acts 14:15)—the same God who raised Jesus from the dead. The natural theology of creation-witness prepares for but does not include the gospel; when the crowd's response prevents more, Paul withdraws with the seed planted: there is a living God whose goodness is evident in creation.

19.synthesis: Jews from Antioch and Iconium arrive, persuade the crowd, and stone Paul—dragging him outside the city, thinking him dead (Acts 14:19). The disciples surround him; he gets up and enters the city. The next day he and Barnabas leave for Derbe (Acts 14:20). They preach the gospel there and make many disciples; then they return through Lystra, Iconium, and Pisidian Antioch, strengthening the souls of the disciples and encouraging them to continue in the faith, for 'through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God' (Acts 14:21–22). They appoint elders in every church with prayer and fasting and commend them to the Lord (Acts 14:23).
19.historical_context: Paul's stoning at Lystra and survival (vv.19–20) may be what he refers to in 2 Corinthians 11:25 ('once I was stoned'). His return through the very cities where he faced opposition to strengthen the new communities is an act of apostolic courage—and pastoral necessity. The appointment of elders (presbyteroi, v.23) is the first institutional act of church-planting in Acts; it reflects the synagogue model of community governance and provides stability for the new communities after the apostles depart. 'Through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom' is Paul's first iteration of a principle that will define his theology of suffering (Romans 8:17; Philippians 1:29).
19.literary_structure: The stoning and survival (vv.19–20a) is the journey's darkest moment and immediately contrasts with the fruitful Derbe mission (v.20b–21). The return journey (vv.21–23) reverses the outward route and converts the mission narrative into a strengthening tour—Paul does not simply plant and leave but returns to consolidate what was planted under persecution. The inclusion of elder-appointment and commendation to the Lord provides closure to the missionary journey's church-planting dimension before the Antioch return report of chapter 14:24–28.
19.theological_application: 'Through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God' (Acts 14:22) is the most concentrated statement of Paul's theology of mission-suffering in Acts. The 'must' (dei) is the divine necessity that runs through Luke-Acts: as it was necessary for the Christ to suffer (Luke 24:26), so it is necessary for his followers. This is not fatalism but teleology: the tribulations are the path through which the kingdom is entered, not the obstacle that prevents it. The church's pastoral care of new communities must include honest preparation for suffering, not the promise of immunity from it.
19.christology: Paul's stoning and survival—dragged out as dead, rising, reentering the city—is Luke's parallel to Jesus' death and resurrection in miniature: the apostle shares the pattern of his Lord. His immediate return to the city is the apostolic courage that flows from the conviction that death does not have the final word. The appointment of elders 'to the Lord in whom they had believed' (v.23) places the new churches under the care of the risen Christ who is their true shepherd; human elders are stewards of what belongs to him.

24.synthesis: Paul and Barnabas travel through Pisidia, Pamphylia, and Perga, speaking the word, then sail to Antioch from Attalia (Acts 14:24–26). When they arrive, they gather the church and report all that God has done with them and how he opened a door of faith to the Gentiles (Acts 14:27). They remain there with the disciples for a long time (Acts 14:28).
24.historical_context: The return to Antioch closes the First Missionary Journey and fulfills the community's commissioning in Acts 13:1–3. The mission report to the gathered church is the first example of missionary accountability to the sending community—a practice that Paul follows throughout Acts (cf. Acts 18:22; Acts 21:17–19). 'Opened a door of faith to the Gentiles' (v.27) anticipates Paul's metaphor in 1 Corinthians 16:9 and 2 Corinthians 2:12—the 'door' of divine opportunity that opens for mission. The extended stay provides rest and theological reflection before the Jerusalem Council of chapter 15.
24.literary_structure: The return report provides narrative closure for the journey while opening the question that chapter 15 will resolve: what does the Gentile mission mean for Jewish-Gentile relations in the church? The summary—'all that God had done with them'—frames the entire journey as divine action through human agents: God opened, God did, God accompanied. This theological passive is characteristic of Acts' understanding of mission: Paul and Barnabas reported what God did; they were the instruments, not the agents.
24.theological_application: The mission report to the home community models the accountability structure that every sent ministry requires. Paul and Barnabas did not act as independent operators but as those sent by and accountable to the Antioch community. The language 'all that God had done with them' is the proper frame for every report of fruitful ministry: not 'what we accomplished' but 'what God did in our midst.' The church community's participation in the mission through sending, prayer, and receiving the returning missionaries' account makes it genuinely a communal enterprise rather than a specialist's work.
24.christology: The 'door of faith to the Gentiles' that God opened through the First Missionary Journey is the risen Christ's own opening: he who holds 'the key of David,' who 'opens and no one will shut' (Revelation 3:7), has opened the way for all peoples to enter his kingdom through faith. The mission narrative of Acts 13–14 is the story of that opening: synagogue by synagogue, city by city, the risen Christ through his apostles creates the community that Isaiah called 'a light to the Gentiles.' The return to Antioch is a breath before the next movement of that great opening. The missionaries' report of 'all that God had done with them' models the proper framing of every ministry account: divine initiative narrated through human participation. The 'door of faith' imagery suggests that the risen Christ is the one who opens what no human institution could provide—access to covenant relationship for all peoples, not through circumcision or ethnicity but through faith in the one who is himself both Lord and door. Antioch receives not a boast about apostolic achievement but a testimony to divine faithfulness across mountain passes, hostile synagogues, and city gates.
